summary: Deep scan of silent catch patterns, duplicate code, missing error boundaries, listener leaks, unguarded JSON.parse, and dead utility code. 20 findings across 6 categories.

findings.title: Sidebar not wrapped in individual ErrorBoundary
findings.description: The desktop Sidebar component (line 197) and mobile MobileDrawer Sidebar (line 307) are rendered without an ErrorBoundary. A crash in sidebar code (e.g. plugin panels) takes down the entire app. Carryover from round 1-2 verdict.
findings.fix: Wrap in Sidebar error}>.

findings.title: TaskDetailPanel not wrapped in individual ErrorBoundary
findings.description: TaskDetailPanel is rendered conditionally (line 357) without its own ErrorBoundary. A crash in the detail panel (comments, relations, markdown) crashes the whole app. Carryover from round 1-2 verdict.
findings.fix: Wrap <TaskDetailPanel .../> in <ErrorBoundary>.

findings.title: AIChatPanel lacks dedicated ErrorBoundary
findings.description: AIChatPanel is rendered in the AIChat view without an ErrorBoundary. The outer view ErrorBoundary in AppLayout catches it, but an AI chat crash shows a generic error instead of a chat-specific recovery UI. Carryover from round 1-2 verdict.
findings.fix: Wrap <AIChatPanel .../> in <ErrorBoundary fallback={<AIChatErrorFallback />}>.

findings.title: LIST_PREFIX regex duplicated in 3 files (shared module exists but unused)
findings.description: The LIST_PREFIX regex is defined in task-extraction-patterns.ts as a shared module, but extract-tasks-from-text.ts (line 227) and ExtractTasksModal.tsx (line 46) still define their own identical copies instead of importing from the shared module.
findings.fix: Import LIST_PREFIX and ACTION_VERBS from src/parser/task-extraction-patterns.ts in both files.

findings.title: fetchWithTimeout implemented 3 times in AI provider adapters
findings.description: Nearly identical fetchWithTimeout helper is defined in lmstudio.ts (line 15), ollama.ts (line 13), and openai-compat.ts (line 187). Only minor signature differences (optional init param). Should be a shared utility.
findings.fix: Extract to src/ai/provider/adapters/fetch-utils.ts or src/utils/fetch.ts and import in all three adapters.

findings.title: Chat message deserialization duplicated despite shared utility existing
findings.description: ai/chat.ts (line 483-491) and ui/api/ai/ai-sessions.ts (line 110-117) both manually deserialize chat message rows with inline JSON.parse(row.toolCalls). The shared ai/message-utils.ts:deserializeChatMessages() was created to replace this pattern but these two call sites were never migrated.
findings.fix: Replace inline deserialization loops with calls to deserializeChatMessages() from ai/message-utils.ts.

findings.title: Unguarded JSON.parse(row.toolCalls) in ai/chat.ts
findings.description: JSON.parse(row.toolCalls) on line 489 is not wrapped in try/catch. If toolCalls contains corrupted JSON (e.g. truncated by storage), this will throw an unhandled error and crash chat session restoration.
findings.fix: Use deserializeChatMessages() from ai/message-utils.ts which handles parse errors gracefully.

findings.title: Unguarded JSON.parse(row.toolCalls) in ui/api/ai/ai-sessions.ts
findings.description: JSON.parse(row.toolCalls) on line 115 is not wrapped in try/catch. Corrupted toolCalls data will crash the session restore in the frontend, leaving the user unable to load chat history.
findings.fix: Wrap in try/catch or use deserializeChatMessages() from ai/message-utils.ts.

findings.title: MicrophoneSection addEventListener not properly cleaned up
findings.description: Line 60 uses status.addEventListener('change', ...) to register a listener, but the cleanup on line 68 sets permStatus.onchange = null. Setting onchange to null does NOT remove listeners added via addEventListener — the listener will persist after unmount.
findings.fix: Store the handler function and call status.removeEventListener('change', handler) in the cleanup function.

findings.title: useSoundEffect silently swallows audio playback errors
findings.description: playSoundRaw().catch(() => {}) silently swallows all errors. While sound is non-critical, logging at debug level would help diagnose why sounds don't play for users who have them enabled.
findings.fix: Change to .catch((err) => logger.debug('Sound playback failed', { error: String(err) })).

findings.title: SoundSection preview silently swallows errors
findings.description: previewSound().catch(() => {}) silently swallows errors when previewing sounds in settings. User clicks preview button and nothing happens with no feedback.
findings.fix: At minimum log the error; ideally show a toast or inline message that the sound preview failed.

findings.title: useGlobalShortcut cleanup silently swallows unregister errors
findings.description: unregisterFn?.().catch(() => {}) silently swallows errors during global shortcut unregistration. If the Tauri API fails to unregister, the shortcut remains active with no indication.
findings.fix: Log at warn level so developers can diagnose shortcut registration issues.

findings.title: useAIChatVoice silently swallows voice.speak errors
findings.description: voice.speak(lastMsg.content).catch(() => {}) silently swallows TTS errors. User expects to hear speech but gets silence with no error indication.
findings.fix: Log at warn level and optionally surface an error state to the voice call UI.

findings.title: SidebarContextMenu clipboard write silently swallows errors
findings.description: navigator.clipboard.writeText(url).catch(() => {}) silently swallows clipboard errors. User selects 'Copy link' from context menu and gets no feedback on failure.
findings.fix: Show a toast notification on failure (e.g. 'Failed to copy link').

findings.title: SettingsContext silently swallows settings load failure
findings.description: .catch(() => { if (mounted) setLoaded(true); }) silently swallows settings load failure. The app will render with default settings but the user has no indication their saved settings were not loaded. At minimum should log.
findings.fix: Add logger.warn('Failed to load settings, using defaults') in the catch handler.

findings.title: PluginBrowser silently swallows registry load failure
findings.description: .catch(() => setLoading(false)) silently swallows plugin registry fetch failures. User sees an empty plugin list with no error state or retry option.
findings.fix: Add an error state and show a message like 'Failed to load plugin registry. Check your connection.' with a retry button.

findings.title: DailyBriefingSection and CustomInstructionsSection silent on load failure
findings.description: Both DailyBriefingSection.tsx:16 and CustomInstructionsSection.tsx:16 use .catch(() => setLoaded(true)) to silently swallow settings load errors. Users may lose their daily briefing or custom instruction settings silently.
findings.fix: Log at warn level so developers can diagnose settings load failures.

findings.title: ThemeManager constructor adds addEventListener without cleanup method
findings.description: ThemeManager adds an event listener to systemThemeQuery in its constructor but has no destroy() or cleanup method. Since it's a singleton (line 90), this is not a memory leak in practice, but it makes the class untestable and prevents proper cleanup if the module is ever hot-reloaded.
findings.fix: Add a destroy() method that calls this.systemThemeQuery.removeEventListener(). Optional for singleton, but good practice.

findings.title: PluginView infinite retry loop with no backoff or max retries
findings.description: The built-in component resolution (lines 82-93) retries every 1000ms with no backoff and no max retry count. If a plugin component never resolves (e.g. removed plugin), this creates an infinite polling loop that runs until the component unmounts.
findings.fix: Add a maxRetries counter (e.g. 10) and stop retrying after the limit, showing an error message instead.

findings.title: useAIMessageActions uses nested setTimeout chains for state synchronization
findings.description: regenerateLastResponse (lines 72-84) uses nested setTimeout(0) calls to synchronize React state updates before re-sending. This pattern (setTimeout inside setTimeout) is fragile and race-prone. It relies on React batching behavior that may change, and the second setTimeout(sendMessage, 0) can fire before the first state update is committed.
findings.fix: Use a useEffect that watches for the truncated state and triggers sendMessage, or use flushSync for the critical state update.
